perturbation.original_text: In the event that either Party should institute proceedings to enforce any provision of this Agreement, the prevailing Party in such proceedings shall be entitled to recover all expenses relating to the enforcement of this Agreement, including reasonable attorneys' fees and costs, in addition to any other remedies.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that either Party should institute proceedings to enforce any provision of this Agreement, the prevailing Party in such proceedings shall be entitled to recover expenses relating to the enforcement of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the provision for reasonable attorneys' fees and costs could violate laws regarding the recovery of such fees in certain types of contract disputes, potentially discouraging parties from enforcing their rights under the agreement. This could be particularly problematic if state law mandates the recovery of attorney's fees in specific contractual situations.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts O.C.G.A. § 13-1-11, which pertains to the enforcement of obligations to pay attorney's fees upon any note or other evidence of indebtedness. While this agreement isn't an 'evidence of indebtedness', similar principles could apply in disputes, and removing the explicit mention creates ambiguity that could be challenged under related case law interpreting the statute. Omitting 'reasonable attorneys' fees and costs' restricts what the prevailing party can recover when O.C.G.A. § 13-1-11 may dictate otherwise.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Georgia Code § 13-1-11 (2023) - Validity and enforcement of obligations to pay attorney's fees upon notes or other evidence of indebtedness :: 2023 Georgia Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Georgia Code 2023 Georgia Code (here) 2022 Georgia Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 CODE OF GEORGIA Title 13 - CONTRACTS (§§ 13-1-1 — 13-12-5) Chapter 1 - GENERAL PROVISIONS (§§ 13-1-1 — 13-1-16) Section 13-1-11 - Validity and enforcement of obligations to pay attorney's fees upon notes or other evidence of indebtedness Universal Citation: GA Code § 13-1-11 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next (a) Obligations to pay attorney's fees upon any note or other evidence of indebtedness, in addition to the rate of interest specified therein, shall be valid and enforceable and collectable as a part of such debt if such note or other evidence of indebtedness is collected by or through an attorney after maturity, subject to subsection (b) of this Code section and to the following provisions: (1) If such note or other evidence of indebtedness provides for attorney's fees in some specific percent of the principal and interest owing thereon, such provision and obligation shall be valid and enforceable up to but not in excess of 15 percent of the principal and interest owing on said note or other evidence of indebtedness; (2) If such note or other evidence of indebtedness provides for the payment of reasonable attorney's fees without specifying any specific percent, such provision shall be construed to mean 15 percent of the first $500.00 of principal and interest owing on such note or other evidence of indebtedness and 10 percent of the amount of principal and interest owing thereon in excess of $500.00; and (3) The holder of the note or other evidence of indebtedness or his o
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits 'reasonable attorneys' fees and costs,' which contradicts the original agreement's provision for recovering all expenses, including these fees. The Law Explanation indicates this omission could be challenged under O.C.G.A. § 13-1-11, and related case law. Although the law snippet (Georgia Code § 13-1-11) specifically addresses 'notes or other evidence of indebtedness,' the Law Explanation suggests similar principles might apply in broader contract disputes. By removing the explicit mention of attorneys' fees, the modified contract weakens the prevailing party's ability to recover these fees, potentially undermining their enforcement rights and creating a loophole that contradicts the intent of the law. The omission directly impacts the enforceability of the original provision, establishing a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges that the Evaluation Material may contain material nonpublic information concerning the Furnishing Party. Each Party further acknowledges its awareness of the restrictions imposed by federal and state securities laws 2 on persons in possession of material nonpublic information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges that the Evaluation Material may contain nonpublic information concerning the Furnishing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific acknowledgement of awareness of federal and state securities laws relating to material nonpublic information weakens the clause. While not a guarantee of compliance, the original text highlights the parties' understanding of their obligations under insider trading laws. The modified version introduces a risk of non-compliance due to the lack of emphasis on legal responsibilities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 § 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_citation: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 § 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts the spirit, if not the letter, of securities laws like the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 § 10(b) and Rule 10b-5, which prohibit the use of material nonpublic information for trading. By removing the explicit acknowledgement of these laws, the agreement becomes less clear about the parties' responsibilities regarding insider information, potentially increasing the risk of violations. While the original text doesn't guarantee compliance, its removal creates a potential legal vulnerability by minimizing the emphasis on adherence to securities regulations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: urity holders. 19 We have considered these views carefully. As discussed in the Proposing Release, we are mindful of the concerns about chilling issuer disclosure; we agree that the market is best served by more, not less, disclosure of information by issuers. Because any potential "chill" is most likely to arise -- if at all -- from the fear of legal liability, we included in proposed Regulation FD significant safeguards against inappropriate liability. Most notably, we stated that the regulation would not provide a basis for private liability, and provided that in Commission enforcement actions under Regulation FD we would need to prove knowing or reckless conduct. 4. Revisions to Narrow the Scope of Regulation FD Nevertheless, to provide even greater protection against the possibility of inappropriate liability, and to guard further against the likelihood of any chilling effect resulting from the regulation, we have modified Regulation FD in several respects. First, we have narrowed the scope of the regulation so that it does not apply to all communications with persons outside the issuer. The regulation will apply only to communications to securities market professionals and to any holder of the issuer's securities under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that the security holder will trade on the basis of the information. Second, we have narrowed the types of issuer personnel covered by the regulation to senior officials and those persons who regularly communicate with securities market professionals or with security holders. The effect of these first two changes is that Regulation FD will not apply to a variety of legitimate, ordinary-course business communications or to disclosures to the media. Third, to remove any doubt that private liability will not result from a Regulation FD violation, we have revised Regulation FD to make absolutely clear that it does not establish a duty for purposes of Rule 10b-5 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 ("Exchange Act"). The regulation now includes an express provision in the text stating that a failure to make a disclosure required solely by Regulation FD will not result in a violation of Rule 10b-5. Fourth, we have made clear that where the regulation speaks of "knowing or reckless" conduct, liability will arise only when an issuer's personnel knows or is reckless in not knowing that the information selectively disclosed is both material and nonpublic. This will provide additional assurance that issuers will not be second-guessed on close materiality judgments. Neither will we, nor could we, bring enforcement actions under Regulation FD for mistaken materiality determinations that were not reckless. Fifth, we have expressly provided that a violation of Regulation FD will not lead to an issuer's loss of eligibility to use short-form registration for a securities offering or affect security holders' ability to resell under Rule 144 under the Securities Act of 1933 ("Securities Act"). This change eliminates additional consequences of a Regulation FD violation that issuers and other commenters considered too onerous. We have made two other significant changes to the scope of Regulation FD, which, while not specifically addressed to concerns about chilling disclosure, narrow its scope. In response to concerns about the interplay of Regulation FD with the Securities Act disclosure regime, we have expressly excluded from the scope of the regulation communications made in connection with most securities offerings registered under the Securities Act. We believe that the Securities Act already accomplishes most of the policy goals of Regulation FD for purposes of registered offerings, and we will consider this topic in the context of a broader Securities Act rulemaking. Also, we have eliminated foreign governments and foreign private issuers from the coverage of the regulation. With these changes, we believe Regulation FD strikes an appropriate balance. It establishes a c
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes an explicit acknowledgement of awareness of federal and state securities laws related to material nonpublic information. The original text highlights the parties' understanding of their obligations under insider trading laws, while the modified version omits this emphasis, increasing the risk of non-compliance with securities regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 addresses concerns about chilling issuer disclosure and includes safeguards against inappropriate liability. By removing the acknowledgement of securities laws, the modified text could be interpreted as weakening safeguards against potential violations, contradicting the intent to avoid chilling disclosure by ensuring awareness of potential liabilities.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the internal laws of the State of Georgia, without regard to the principles of the conflict of laws thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Georgia.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "without regard to the principles of the conflict of laws thereof," the agreement introduces potential ambiguity regarding which state's laws will apply if the parties are located in different states or if the agreement has connections to other jurisdictions. This could lead to disputes over which state's laws govern the agreement, potentially violating legal principles governing choice of law and jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: Omitting the clause 'without regard to the principles of the conflict of laws thereof' directly contradicts established principles of conflict of laws, such as those articulated in the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187. This section deals with the law of the state chosen by the parties and how that law is chosen. It outlines the conditions under which the parties' choice of law will be honored and when it may be disregarded. By removing this consideration, the agreement neglects to address situations where Georgia law might conflict with the laws of another jurisdiction with a more significant relationship to the parties or the transaction, thereby creating potential legal challenges related to jurisdiction and applicable law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The omission of 'without regard to the principles of the conflict of laws thereof' introduces ambiguity and potential legal challenges, contradicting established principles of conflict of laws. As stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, 'Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute.' By removing the clause, the agreement neglects to address situations where Georgia law might conflict with the laws of another jurisdiction, potentially leading to disputes and making the contract non-compliant with standard legal practices regarding choice of law. This aligns with the Omission Legal Contradiction definition, where essential information is removed, creating legal loopholes.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party consents and submits to the exclusive jurisdiction of the federal and state courts in the State of Georgia, and the city of Atlanta, for the adjudication of any action, suit, or proceeding arising out of or otherwise relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party consents and submits to the jurisdiction of the state courts in the State of Georgia for the adjudication of any action, suit, or proceeding arising out of or otherwise relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the reference to federal courts weakens the agreement's enforceability. This omission could be problematic if a claim arises under federal law, as it may lead to disputes about the proper forum for resolving such claims. It contradicts principles of federal jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: This edit contradicts 28 U.S. Code § 1331, which grants federal district courts original jurisdiction over civil actions arising under the Constitution, laws, or treaties of the United States. By removing the explicit consent to federal jurisdiction, the agreement may impede a party's ability to bring a federal question claim in federal court, even if such jurisdiction is proper. This creates a potential legal risk because the agreement might be interpreted as an attempt to limit access to federal courts in cases where federal law clearly provides a basis for jurisdiction. While it might not explicitly bar a federal case, its omission introduces ambiguity and could lead to jurisdictional disputes.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit consent to federal jurisdiction, which directly contradicts 28 U.S. Code § 1331. This law, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 2, grants federal district courts original jurisdiction over civil actions arising under the Constitution, laws, or treaties of the United States. By omitting the reference to federal courts, the contract impedes a party's ability to bring a federal question claim in federal court, creating a direct contradiction.
